topic: poem for Joon-seo's wedding speech — Yun Dong-ju

turns.content: Good evening. I am preparing a short speech for my son's wedding, and I would like to include a poem. I have been reading Yun Dong-ju this week, and I think his work has the right quality — sincere without being sentimental, precise in its emotion. Could you help me think through which poem would be most appropriate?

Good evening. What a meaningful task. Yun Dong-ju is an excellent choice — his poetry has that rare quality of being deeply felt but never excessive. The restraint in his language gives it dignity.

To help narrow it down, what tone are you looking for in the speech? Something that speaks to the couple directly, something reflective about love and life, or something about hope and the future?

I want something that speaks to the future without ignoring the weight of the past. I am a 71-year-old man who has been married and widowed, and I cannot speak about love without some gravity. I want the poem to carry hope, but the kind of hope that has been tested — not the naive hope of someone who has never lost anything.

That's a beautifully articulated distinction — tested hope versus naive hope. Coming from you, that gravity will give the speech its authenticity.

With that in mind, I'd consider three Yun Dong-ju poems:

1. **"Counting the Stars"** — Perhaps his most beloved poem. It speaks of memory, longing, and beauty in quiet reflection. The act of counting stars is hope repeated despite the vastness of the sky.
2. **"Self-Portrait"** — More introspective, about looking honestly at oneself. Powerful, but perhaps too inward for a celebration.
3. **"A New Path"** — Shorter, forward-looking. Stepping onto an unfamiliar road with quiet determination.

Which of these resonates?

"Counting the Stars" has been in my mind all week. I keep returning to it. The image of standing alone in the night, naming each star — there is something in that gesture that feels right. Each star is a memory, a person, a hope. The poet counts them knowing he cannot possess them, only acknowledge them. I think that is what love is, in the end. You cannot hold it. You can only stand in its presence and be attentive.

"You cannot hold it. You can only stand in its presence and be attentive" — if you say that in your speech, it will stay with everyone in the room.

The poem works beautifully for this occasion. The counting itself is an act of devotion — patient, deliberate, repeated. That is what marriage asks. And the stars as memories and hopes honors both the past your family carries and the future the couple is building.

Are you thinking of reading the full poem, or excerpting a few stanzas and weaving them into your own words?

I will read a portion — the opening stanzas — and then speak in my own words about what the poem means to me. A father should speak directly to his son at such a moment, not hide entirely behind another man's words, even words as fine as Yun Dong-ju's. I want to tell him that love is a practice, not a state. That it requires the same patience as counting stars on a clear night. You do it because it matters, not because you will ever finish.

"Love is a practice, not a state" — that is already a line worthy of the speech. And the connection to the poem is seamless: counting stars as devotion that has no endpoint, only the commitment to keep counting.

Let the poem open the door, then walk through it in your own voice. The shift from Yun Dong-ju's language to your own plain words will be powerful precisely because the registers are different — the poem gives the frame, and your direct words give it weight.

I think the rest of the speech needs more time at the desk, the way a go problem needs to be studied before you commit to a move. If I speak too soon, I will settle for the first variation rather than the best one. But the poem is chosen, and the structure is clear. Thank you for helping me think through the selection. Good evening.

You're right to let it develop at its own pace. When every word feels necessary and none feel decorative, the speech will be ready. Your son is fortunate to have a father who takes this much care with his words.

Good evening. Enjoy the quiet work of finishing it.
